data.title: China's Gold Market Update: Central Bank Purchases Continue In January
data.description:
Looking ahead

  • We anticipate continued strength in bar and coin demand while the soaring gold price may weigh on gold jewellery sales in tonnage terms – although consumer spending may not change much. And in the longer-term, a pilot programme that allows Chinese insurers to buy physical gold should provide additional support for investment demand.
  • And judging from the surging attention consumers are paying to gold, there is a possibility that wholesale demand in the near term – driven mainly by investment purchases – may stay stable before it cools as Q2, historically an off-season of gold consumption in China, approaches.

Gold kicked off 2025 with a sizable gain

Gold prices surged during the first month of 2025: the LBMA gold price PM in USD jumped 8% while the SHAUPM in RMB rose 5% (Chart 1). A strengthening local currency and fewer trading days due to the Chinese New Year (CNY) holiday between 28 January and 4 February led to the relative underperformance of the RMB gold price.

Our analysis shows that heightened geopolitical risks – such as the Trump administration’s tariff policies – improving gold ETF inflows and rekindling inflation concerns were main contributors to the record-shattering gold price in January.

Chart 1: Gold starts 2025 on a strong note

Annual returns of the SHAUPM in RMB and the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD*

Wholesale demand improved m/m but stayed weak y/y

China’s gold demand improved during the CNY month. On the wholesale side, 125t of gold were shipped out from the SGE, a 3% m/m rise (Chart 2). Seasonal stock replenishment from jewellery retailers, banks and other market participants ahead of the CNY holiday – a traditional peak season for gold consumption in China – was a main contributor.

Chart 2: Seasonal replenishment drives up wholesale demand m/m

Gold withdrawals from the SGE and the Shanghai-London gold price spread*

But our pre-holiday field research in Shenzhen, the hub of China’s gold jewellery wholesaling and manufacturing, indicates weaker-than-usual sentiment among gold jewellery retailers. With the gold price soaring and the past year’s demand picture reminding them of potential sustained weaknesses, gold jewellery retailers lowered their expectations for the holiday sales and stocked up less than previous years. And this is also reflected in the 54% y/y decline in January’s wholesale gold demand – but it is important to note that 2024 saw the strongest January in history – and it was 37% below the ten-year average (Chart 3).

Chart 3: Yet the seasonal m/m improvement can’t mask the y/y weakness

Gold withdrawals from the SGE and the 10-year average*

Chinese gold ETF flows flipped negative in January

Chinese gold ETFs lost RMB2.8bn (US$399mn) in January, equivalent to a 4.7t reduction in holdings (Chart 4). After the month’s outflow, their total AUM fell to RMB70bn (US$9.8bn) and collective holdings reached 110t. We believe the outflow can be mainly attributed to profit-taking activities ahead of the CNY holiday to avoid additional volatilities from international markets while the local market is closed.1 Meanwhile, improving investor risk appetite amid the stronger-than-expected Q4 and 2024 GDP data releases in the month may also have led to outflows from gold, the safe-haven asset.2

Chart 4: Chinese gold ETFs saw outflows in January

Collective holdings and monthly changes of Chinese gold ETFs*

China’s official gold holdings rose three months in a row

The PBoC announced another gold purchase to start 2025: the 5t increase lifted China’s official gold holdings to 2,285t, accounting for 5.9% of total foreign reserves (Chart 5). As noted in our recently published Gold Demand Trends report, China announced a total of 44t gold purchases during 2024 despite its six-month pause in the middle of the year. And we believe the PBoC’s move could have a positive impact on sentiment among local gold investors, as indicated by past anecdotal evidence.

Chart 5: The PBoC reported its third consecutive monthly gold purchase in January

Reported official gold holdings and gold as a percentage of total foreign exchange reserves*

Gold imports ended 2024 with a y/y decline

December saw China import 84t of gold, concluding Q4 with a total of 270t, based on the most recent data from China Customs. While Q4 imports fell 14% y/y, they rose more than 160% q/q – the y/y weakness and q/q seasonal strength are in line with our observation of China’s gold demand during the quarter.

In 2024, China imported 1,225t of gold (Chart 6), a 14% y/y decline and 16% below the pre-COVID five-year average (1,460t). And we saw similar trends in China’s gold consumption in 2024, which fell 10% compared to 2023, leading to the decrease in imports.

Chart 6: Gold imports remain below pre-COVID levels

Gold imports and averages pre-COVID*

Looking ahead

The CNY holiday between late January and early February showed signs of consumption revival. For instance, box office revenues during the holiday surged to a historical high and consumer spending on dining and travelling both rose compared to the same period in 2024.3

Gold consumption was also booming. Various information indicates that gold jewellery stores were busy during the holiday. Meanwhile, the climbing gold price continued to push consumers to lighter products.4

And the investment story is a much stronger one. The strong gold price performance, the central bank’s continued purchasing announcements and the local currency volatilities kept lifting investor attention for gold. Currently, the online searches for gold topped their previous peak seen in 2013 (Chart 7), when gold demand in China surged to the highest in history. Our conversations with market participants indicate that gold bar sales maintained their stunning pace seen in 2024, even leading to inventory shortages for some.

Chart 7: Consumer sentiment towards gold reached a record high

Baidu Search Index of the keyword “Gold”*

We believe the current trend may continue. In tonnage terms, demand for gold jewellery may stay tepid – although value-preserving motives will provide some support – but bar and coin sales should remain hot – and any gold price adjustment could be viewed as a good opportunity to enter. Lastly, we believe a recent announcement of policy changes that allows ten Chinese insurers to buy up to 1% of each company’s total assets in physical gold, as a pilot run, should provide longer-term support for local investment gold demand.5

Footnotes

1Local financial markets were closed due to the CNY holiday between 28 January and 4 February 2025.

2For more, see: China's fourth-quarter GDP grows at 5.4%, beating market expectations | 16 January 2025.

3For more, see: 消费迎来“开门红”！春节期间零售和餐饮同比增长4.1%，以旧换新首批810亿元资金已下达_腾讯新闻 | 8 February 2025.

4For more, see: 2025春节黄金市场热潮：小克重产品爆红，消费者观望情绪升温_金价_变化_央行| 7 February 2025.

5For more, see: China launches pilot program for insurance fund investments in gold - Chinadaily.com.cn| 8 February 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: How To Prepare For Stagflation
data.description:
Inflation is as violent as a mugger, as frightening as an armed robber and as deadly as a hit man." - Ronald Reagan

Equities had a good trading week before the holiday weekend. The NASDAQ led the way with a rise of 2.6% while the S&P 500 was up 1.5%. The Dow was up only .6% on the week and the small-cap Russell 2000 was flat, however.

The rally in the overall market was impressive given increasing concerns around a Tariff War erupting. In addition, stocks rose even as the markets got more signs during the week that inflation is becoming somewhat intractable and economic growth also seems to be slowing. The probability that the economy is entering a period of stagflation is increasing, investors should not ignore the rising chances of this economic scenario unfolding. It could be quite a negative backdrop for stock portfolios.

Let's take a look at the two big inflationary signs that hit the market last week in the form of the January CPI and PPI reports. Last Wednesday, the Consumer Price Index or CPI rose 3.0 percent from the same period a year ago as food and energy prices picked up. On Thursday, the January PPI reading showed that wholesale prices increased more than expected as the Producer Price Index rose 0.4% in January. Unadjusted PPI increased to 3.5% over the past year, while core PPI which excludes food and energy rose 3.4%.

Both inflation readings strengthened the case for the Federal Reserve to continue to hit the pause button on any further cuts to the Fed Funds rate until inflation levels move much closer to the central bank's two percent target. As previously mentioned, there is much speculation that a more aggressive tariff policy from the new administration will potentially push inflation levels higher.

In addition to recent readings showing inflation to be quite intractable and stubbornly above the Federal Reserve's official two percent target, economic growth seems to be weakening. Yes, huge tech companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Alphabet (GOOG) are projected to spend more than $300 billion to build AI data centers and infrastructure in 2025. Outside this development, growth prospects for the U.S. economy are looking quite iffy at the moment.

Recently, the initial estimate of Q4 GDP growth was revised down from 2.6% to 2.3%. This was also down significantly from the 3.1% level in Q3. In addition, a decent amount of this growth is driven by government spending, which is up in the low teens over the first four months of the federal government's fiscal 2025 year. Revenues are slightly down of note from the same period a year ago, another sign of a tepid economy. The fiscal deficit in these four months was just under $840 billion, compared to $531 billion in the four months of FY2024. This follows a fiscal deficit of just north of $1.8 trillion in FY2024 during an economic expansion. With the Federal debt to U.S. GDP ratio near all-time highs, clearly, this is unsustainable over the long term.

The January BLS Jobs report showed that 143,000 positions were created during the month. This was some 25,000 in light of consensus estimates. The new administration also seems quite determined to pare the federal workforce significantly as well. Late last week, January retail sales dropped by .9% on a month-over-month basis. This is far worse than the negative .2% decline expected and the steepest monthly drop in two years. A sign that consumer demand is tepid.

Then we have the housing sector, which historically accounts for 15% to 18% of overall U.S. economic activity. Average mortgage rates have more than doubled since the start of 2022. Combined with home price appreciation, this has pushed housing affordability near all-time lows. This led to the lowest levels of existing home sales in both 2023 and 2024 since 1995. With the average 30-year mortgage hovering near seven percent, 2025 is very likely to be another punk year for existing home sales.

Home builders were filling the void of supplying demand, given existing homeowners were quite reluctant to give up their three to four percent mortgage rate on their existing properties for most of the 2022-2024 period. However, home builders like Lennar (LEN) are now seeing additional headwinds as housing inventory levels have started to surge over the last few quarters. This led to additional incentives like mortgage rate buydowns and free upgrades to move inventory. These increased incentives have driven profit margins lower across the home building sector. This is a key reason that the SPDR® S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB) is down more than 15% from its recent highs in late November.

Stubborn inflation and slowing economic growth add up to stagflation, an economic scenario that seems to be becoming more likely by the month. Given where the S&P 500 is trading at based on historical valuation metrics, this makes the overall market quite vulnerable to significant pullback if Stagflation does set in across the economy in the quarters ahead.

Based on the stagflation experienced in the U.S. from the late 1970s to the early 1980s, commodities and hard assets are likely to outperform the overall market as investors look for investments that benefit from stubborn inflation. Defensive areas like healthcare also outperformed during that stagflationary period, it should be noted. Gold prices have moved up nicely in the first few weeks of 2025. This has benefited the gold-related stocks in my portfolio, like Barrick Gold Corporation (GOLD) and Newmont Corporation (NEM). There was a solid new bullish article on the latter here on Seeking Alpha 10 days ago.

I also recently started to build a new position in The St. Joe Company (JOE) as the substantial acreage of land the company holds in northwest Florida appears significantly undervalued. Something an article on Seeking Alpha highlighted some five weeks ago.

In conclusion, given the growing threat of Stagflation setting in across the economy in the coming quarters, I remain cautious about the overall market. About a third of my portfolio is in short-term treasuries. Most of the rest is within covered call holdings on the relatively few stocks I am finding that still sport reasonable valuations.

data.title: AstraZeneca Post 2024 Earnings: Unwanted Headwinds Warrant Downgrade To Hold
data.description:
Investment Overview

Earlier this month, AstraZeneca PLC (NASDAQ:AZN) reported its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings, beating street estimates for top and bottom-line performance.

In Q4, revenues came to $14.89bn, up 25% year-on-year, operating profit came to $2bn, up 79%, and earnings per share ("EPS") came to $0.97, up 71%. For the full year, total revenues amounted to $54.1bn, up 21% year-on-year, reported operating profit was $10bn, up 32%, and reported EPS was $4.54, up 29%. Core operating profit was reported as $16.93bn, up 22% year-on-year, and core EPS $8.21, up 19%.

AstraZeneca is not resting on its laurels, either, forecasting for total revenues to increase in 2025 by a "high single digit" percentage, and core EPS to grow by a "low double-digit" percentage (source: earnings presentation). Management increased its dividend payout by 7% in 2024, and will increase it by a further 7% in 2025 - the current annual yield is just over 2%.

In terms of its indebtedness, on its call with analysts to discuss results, the Chief Financial Officer told analysts:

We completed a number of business development transactions in 2024 including the acquisition of Amolyt, Icosavax and Fusion and incurred total debt payments close to $7 billion. We anticipate deal payments relating to past transactions of approximately $3 billion in 2025.

Net debt increased by $2.1 billion to $24.6 billion, which is a level we are comfortable with. Given the growth in our EBITDA, our current net debt-to-EBITDA ratio stands at 1.5 times.

By Big Pharma standards, the level of leverage is perfectly acceptable - rival Pharma Amgen (AMGN), for example, has a ratio of >10x, while the sector average is ~2-3x, I'd speculate.

Away from the financials, in Q4, AstraZeneca secured approvals for two new molecular entities. Kavigale received EU approval for the prevention of COVID-19 in immunocompromised people aged 12 years and older. Datroway was approved in the US to treat patients with HER-2 negative breast cancer who have received prior endocrine-based therapy and chemotherapy for unresectable or metastatic disease.

In total, the Big Pharma achieved approvals for eight "new molecular entities" ("NMEs") in 2024, the others being cancer meds Truqap (breast), and Imjudo (liver), RSV vaccine Beyfortus, Wainua (polyneuropathy), Airsupra (asthma), and Voydeya (add-on for paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuric treatment).

The company reported nine "positive, high-value Phase 3 trial readouts" in 2024, and expects to secure 20 NME approvals between 2022 and 2030, enabling it to achieve $80bn revenues in 2030, with a "mid-30" percentage operating margin.

Analysis: Strong Performance Undermined By ADC Woes, China Probe

If we consider AstraZeneca's 2025 guidance, we might calculate that all being well, revenues in 2025 will come to ~$58.5bn, and core EPS ~$9.3, which translates to a forward price to sales ("P/S") ratio of ~3.9x, and a forward price to earnings ratio of ~8x. Both of these figures are well below the Big Pharma sector average, and should, by rights, be interpreted as strong "Buy" signals.

After I previewed AstraZeneca's Q2 earnings for Seeking Alpha back in July, giving the company's stock a "Buy" rating, shares quickly increased in value from $78 per share, to a high of ~$88 by late August - up ~13%, but then they began to slide.

There was nothing wrong with Q2 results - revenues of $12.94bn, up >13% year-on-year, operating income of $10.8bn, up >14%, but the Pharma did report disappointing Phase 3 study results for its antibody drug conjugate Dato DX (now approved as Datroway in breast cancer as discussed above) in lung cancer, in which it was unable to extend life longer than chemotherapy docetaxel by a statistically significant amount. This was followed by the news in September that the drug's final data in breast cancer failed to meet its endpoint of progression-free survival, again failing to beat chemo (although its safety profile was superior).

That took the sheen off the Pharma's recent performance, but worse was to come. In September last year, AstraZeneca revealed the President of its China business and four other members of senior management were under investigation by the Chinese authorities in a probe apparently related to "violation of data privacy laws and illegal importation of unapproved drugs."

China is a key market for AstraZeneca - the Pharma earned $6.4bn in the region in 2024, with revenues increasing by 11% year-on-year, however the figure would have been higher had it not been for a 3% decline in revenues in Q4, illustrating the negative effect the investigation is already having on business.

On the Q4 earnings call with analysts, AstraZeneca's CEO told them:

We've received a notification from the Shenzhen City Customs Office regarding suspected unpaid importation taxes totaling $900,000, which to the best of our knowledge relates to Imfinzi and Imjudo. So if actually AstraZeneca was found liable, a fine of between one to five times the amount of avoided import duties could have to be paid by AstraZeneca. We are continuing to cooperate with the government towards resolution of those investigations.

The Pharma's Head of International also commented:

while we do expect some headwinds specifically driven by VVP inclusion for our several medicines, we do expect the growth in China will continue and especially in the longer term where we see a strong opportunity and we are fully committed to continue growing in China.

The company's former Head of International remains detained in China, and AZN has said it is unable to speak with him. As well as Imfinzi and Imjudo, which are not formally approved in China and which Chinese authorities apparently believe may have been illegally imported from Hong Kong (it accuses AZN of illegally storing patient data), AstraZeneca may receive fines in relation to Enhertu, its breast cancer med.

The issues in China were primarily responsible for dragging AstraZeneca stock from its August high, to a low of ~$63 by mid-November, wiping out all the recent gains driven by its overall outperformance as a business.

Looking Ahead: On The Comeback Trail, Or Stuck In The Mire?

Certainly, AstraZeneca's share price performance this year - up >13% - suggests the company has been riding out the China crisis and ought not feel any long-term effects. However, at a time when "Big Pharma" is under intense scrutiny, not only in China, but in Europe and the US, the ongoing situation is a "bad look" for the company that has undermined faith in its senior management.

Still, if we look ahead at business developments expected in 2025, there are plenty of upcoming catalysts that can help to lighten the gloom.

As we can see above, the Pharma expects to deliver 7 Phase 3 data readouts for NMEs in 2025, and secure label expansions for other key assets. However, we might point to the fact that at least two of these involve Datroway, which, as discussed above, has failed to statistically significant outperformance over chemo in both breast and lung cancers.

AstraZeneca is undeniably an oncology powerhouse, earning $22.3bn, or 41% of its revenues from this division in 2024, however, the ADC setbacks have made me revise my previously shared forecasts for the division's long-term performance as per the table below:

The table still presents an optimistic scenario whereby most of AstraZeneca's pipeline assets (highlighted green, patent expiries highlighted red) secure approval and are driving "blockbuster" revenues by 2030, and the division grows at a CAGR of 16% and is worth almost $50bn per annum by 2030.

If we consider AstraZeneca's biopharmaceuticals division, patent expiries for key assets such as Farxiga, a >$7.5bn selling drug in 2024, will affect revenues negatively long term, although AstraZeneca has promised further growth in 2025, despite its looming loss of exclusivity. Still, I see these divisions generating less revenue over time.

Finally, new product approvals in rare disease can drive strong growth in that division, I'd anticipate, although there will be major patent expiries potentially to contend with.

As we can see in the final line, my forecasts indicate that AstraZeneca can drive mid-to-high single digit percentage growth in 2025, and >$80bn revenues by 2030. However, I would stress the tables are for illustrative purposes only, mapping out an optimistic scenario, and I am somewhat skeptical that all approval shots will come off, or that the rates of growth I am forecasting are truly achievable.

Concluding Thoughts: Is AstraZeneca Stock A "Buy," "Sell" or "Hold" Post 2024 Earnings / 2025 Guidance?

When I gave AstraZeneca stock a "Hold" rating in May last year, it was because I was skeptical about management achieving its target >$80bn revenues per annum by 2030. Since that note, due to the issues in China primarily, the share price is down 5%, but we may speculate if it weren't for China, stock might still be riding high at ~$85 per share, and stock would be up >10%.

In a sense, then, we could ask, should we buy AstraZeneca stock today based on its potential? Hold it in the knowledge that ultimately, projected revenues are not the same as actual revenues, but in the expectation that losses due to the China situation are likely to be recovered? Or, sell the stock on the premise that AstraZeneca's recent actions have let down investors, and also because some of its more recent oncology data readouts have been suspect, undermining the promise of its most important division?

Ultimately, readers will make up their own minds. My conclusion is that I would prefer to play a "wait and see" game, as, to my mind, I don't see the current share price as undervaluing the company even with the low P/E and P/S metrics suggesting otherwise.

There are many headwinds buffeting Big Pharma in 2025, such as the Inflation Reduction Act and controls on drug pricing FX uncertainty, a new healthcare administration in the US, tariffs, and fundamental issues undermining the ability of health insurers and pharmacy benefit managers to service patients adequately.

Throw in problems of AstraZeneca's own making, and a "Hold" rating, a downgrade for me personally, is the conclusion I come to. I also don't find the catalysts shares for 2025 as compelling as management, and don't anticipate as many new product approvals in 2025, although these may arrive en masse in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron Technology: Growth Potential In The AI Era
data.description:
This year, I have started expanding coverage into names in the semiconductor industry and I believe that with the growth of AI and trade and technology turmoil globally, the semiconductor industry has a lot of growth potential ahead. In this report, I will be adding Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) to my coverage. I will be discussing the company’s most recent earnings, and the outlook for Q2 FY25, and provide an assessment of the risks and opportunities as well as a price target for the stock.

Micron Technology Sees Huge Improvement In Results

Micron Technology saw a huge increase in its sales compared to the comparable period last year. Revenues grew 84% to $8.71 billion while gross profits grew from $37 million to $3.4 billion and operating income improved from a $955 million loss to a $2.4 billion profit. The huge swing in results is driven by huge demand for chips, while in the comparable period last year, there was an oversupply of memory chips. Sequentially, revenues grew 12% while gross margins expanded 22% and operating income grew 37.2%. DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) sales accounted for 73% of the sales while NAND accounted for 26% of the sales with 1% for other sales. DRAM revenues increased 20% driven by volumes and pricing sequentially while NAND sales declined 5% driven by lower volumes and pricing. So, DRAM and NAND have different market dynamics. The growth is driven by data center sales and smartphone volumes, while PC and automotive trends were weak.

Compute and Networking saw sales increase sequentially by 44%, while the Mobile segment saw sales decline 19% and Embedded and Storage sales were down 10% and up 3%, respectively.

Micron Technology Guides For Lower Sales

For the second quarter, Micron Technology is guiding for $7.7 billion to $8.1 billion in sales which provides a $800 million to $1 billion lower revenue compared to Q1. The company expects DRAM and NAND inventories to be up in Q2 and that explains the revenue outlook.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Micron Technology?

Micron Technology has opportunities driven by growth in data centers and mobile sales. However, there are some weak spots, including sales for PC and automotive. The automotive sector is going through a difficult time and that has its effect on suppliers. Generally, NAND solutions are perfect for the automotive industry, and with stiff competition from China and the automotive industry turmoil NAND sales and pricing are under pressure. What also plays a role is that chips are getting more powerful, leading to transient impacts on demand, which become more profound if demand is already on the weaker side. For DRAM, node transitions offer an opportunity. The 1-Beta node launched in 2022 still uses deep ultraviolet multiple pattern technologies, but offers significantly higher bit density compared to 1-Alpha. The company is now working on the production ramp-up for the 1-gamma node, which uses EUV technology which allows an even higher bit density.

So, overall, we see that there are big opportunities but particularly in the NAND space there are some pressures. The company has to also keep in mind that as it transitions nodes, the bit density becomes higher, and to keep sales volumes up the increase in bit density has to match the market growth.

Micron Technology Has Upside For FY26

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I believe that Micron Technology is a buy. The stock is undervalued compared to peers, and while it's overvalued with FY25 earnings in mind, the FY26 outlook provides a 21% upside with a $128.77 price target. Furthermore, the company has a cash pile that's expected to increase in the years to come, which the company could use to align capex with bit density if needed or return the value to shareholders. That's a flexibility I do like. EBITDA is expected to grow by 41.6% annually between 2024 and 2027 while free cash flow is expected to increase from $121 million in 2024 and $3.1 billion in 2025 (estimated) to $6 billion in 2026 and $5 billion in 2027. With this assessment, my price target does not differ much from that of Wall Street analysts, who have a $130.42 average price target for MU stock.

Conclusion: Micron Technology Stock Is A Buy

I believe that Micron Technology stock is a buy. One thing that we should keep in mind is that in case there are soft spots in demand for DRAM and particularly NAND, we could see soft pricing and volumes pressuring the revenues and cash flows and the company will have to pace its capex accordingly in order to not flood the market with higher bit density chips resulting in volume pressure. So, the outlook appears to be good but the company has to constantly assess the growth in its end markets.

data.title: Buy And Hold These Ten Cheap Lithium Juniors Ready For A Potential Lithium Recovery From 2026 Onwards
data.description:
The lithium market is still experiencing a severe bear market with lithium prices well below incentive prices for many junior miners & producers. This results in many new projects being delayed and many existing project expansions also being delayed.

Meanwhile plugin EV sales (up ~25% in 2024) and battery energy stationary storage ("BESS") sales (up 53% in 2024) are still booming; which if it continues, would lead to a large tidal wave of lithium demand ahead and potentially large lithium deficits starting later this decade.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 76,150 (~USD 10,460) (source)

A lithium demand tidal wave is potentially coming later this decade

Looking at the Trend Investing lithium demand v supply model it takes just one major project to be delayed to have a major impact on the results. For example, if Zijin Mining [SSE:601899] (OTCPK:ZIJMF) choose to further delay their 3Q Project ("Tres Quebradas") in Argentina (it has been delayed once already) it brings the lithium market back into balance.

We know new lithium projects have a very long history of being delayed, usually by 2-3 years. Zijin Mining is a copper and gold producer, so lithium is not yet important to them and a two year delay is not really significant for them. We could also say the same for Rio Tinto (RIO) who are in the process of acquiring Arcadium Lithium.

Zijin postponed the startup of projects in Argentina and Tibet - January 2025 (source)

As shown below the lithium market would quickly move to be roughly balanced in 2026-2028 and then move to massively growing deficits from 2029+ unless new supply is rapidly secured.

Trend Investing lithium demand v supply model assuming Zijin's 3Q Project is delayed by 2 years

Note: The numbers show the market balance, surplus or in brackets deficit.

The take away here is that it really will only take one or two new projects to be delayed for the lithium market to balance and more significantly lead to huge deficits later this decade.

Many investors have no idea the huge lithium demand wave that potentially lies ahead. Just look at the demand growth numbers in our model below for 2025 onwards (green shaded background). In 2030 lithium demand will potentially increase in just one year by almost the entire size of the industry in 2024.

Note: These are model numbers and are therefore only a forecast; however up until now Trend Investing's forecasts on EV sales and lithium demand have been right on the mark.

Note: McKinsey and the Global Battery Alliance forecast a 27% compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") on demand for lithium-ion batteries, reaching 4,700 GWh by 2030 (source page 11). At 0.85kg Li/kWh 4,700 GWh by 2030 equates to 3.995 million tonnes lithium demand by 2030 (by end 2029). Our model above has the end 2029 lithium total demand at 3.853 million tonnes (a similar result).

If you believe that EV and BESS adoption will continue to grow rapidly each year then the above numbers give a great guide as to the massive demand and potential deficits that lie ahead.

This is the opportunity for nimble junior lithium miners that can fill a potential tight supply market from 2026 and a huge potential supply gap from 2029 onwards.

Ten cheap lithium juniors ready for a potential lithium recovery from 2026 onwards

Below is a table of the cheap 'quality' lithium juniors with potential to be near term producers this decade. All have good lithium projects in relatively safe jurisdictions (Australia, Canada, Brazil, Argentina).

Potential production start Market cap (US$m) stock price
Atlas Lithium (ATLX) ~Late 2025 92 US$5.97
Galan Lithium [ASX:GLN] (OTCPK:GLNLF) H2, 2025- H1, 2026 58 A$0.12
Lithium Ionic Corp. [TSXV:LTH] (OTCQX:LTHCF) ~H2, 2026 112 C$1.00
Critical Elements Lithium [TSXV:CRE] (OTCQX:CRECF) ~2027 85 C$0.55
Frontier Lithium [TSXV:FL] (OTCQX:LITOF) ~H1, 2027 84 C$0.53
Winsome Resources [ASX:WR1] (OTCQB:WRSLF) ~2028 44 A$0.31
Patriot Battery Metals [TSX:PMET] [ASX:PMT] (OTCQX:PMETF) ~2029 304 C$2.74
Global Lithium Resources [ASX:GL1](OTCPK:GBLRF) ~2030 33 A$0.20
Lithium South Development Corp. [TSXV:LIS] (OTCQB:LISMF)/POSCO ~2030 10 C$0.13
Argentina Lithium & Energy [TSXV:LIT] ~2030+ 10 C$0.11

Note: The links above to the estimated production date start will mostly direct you to the latest 'company presentations'. These dates are subject to change and will depend upon various milestones being achieved, notably in most cases project funding.

There are other lithium juniors to consider such as Volt Lithium [CVE:VLT] (OTCQB:VLTLF), Atlantic Lithium [LSE:ALL] [ASX:A11] (OTCQX:ALLIF), Standard Lithium [TSXV:SLI] (SLI), ioneer Ltd [ASX:INR] [GR:4G1] (IONR), Lithium Americas [TSX:LAC] (LAC), Sibanye Stillwater [JSE:SSW] (SBSW), Vulcan Energy Resources [ASX:VUL] (OTCPK:VULNF), Savannah Resources [LSE:SAV] [GR:SAV] (OTCPK:SAVNF), European Lithium Ltd [ASX:EUR] (OTCQB:EULIF)/ Critical Metals Corp (CRML), Delta Lithium [ASX:DLI], Q2 Metals Corp. [TSXV:QTWO] (QTWO), Avalon Advanced Materials [TSX:AVL] (OTCQX:AVLNF), European Metals Holdings [ASX:EMH] [AIM:EMH] [GR:E861] (OTCQX:EMHLF) (OTCQX:EMHXY), E3 Lithium Ltd. [TSXV:ETL] [FSE:OW3] (OTCQX:EEMMF), Century Lithium [TSXV:LCE] (OTCQX:CYDVF), Nevada Lithium Corp. [TSXV:NVLH] [FSE:87K](OTCQB:NVLHF), Snow Lake Lithium (LITM), Green Technology Metals [ASX: GT1], Wildcat Resources [ASX:WC8], Lake Resources NL [ASX:LKE] [GR:LK1] (OTCQB:LLKKF), American Lithium Corp. [TSXV:LI] [FSE:5LA1] (OTC:AMLIF), Wealth Minerals [TSXV:WML] [GR:EJZN] (OTCQB:WMLLF), and St George Mining Ltd. [ASX:SGQ] [GR:SOG] etc that can be considered.

Please see our "Lithium junior monthly news" for a more complete summary and latest news for each company. It should be noted also that Rio Tinto [ASX:RIO] [LN:RIO] (RIO) is making big moves into the lithium sector.

Risks

  • The assumptions in our model may not come true.
  • Lithium supply could continue to exceed demand, thereby limiting the opportunity for lithium juniors.
  • Mining risks - Exploration risks, funding risks, permitting risks, development and production risks.
  • Sovereign risk - Low in Canada and Australia. Moderate in Brazil and Argentina.
  • Company risks - Management, debt, liquidity, currency risks.
  • Stock market risks - Dilution, lack of liquidity (best to buy on local exchange), market sentiment.

Note: Junior miners are inherently risky due to a combination of many of the risks above.

Further reading

  • Jan. 2025 - Global electric vehicle sales up 25% in record 2024
  • Jan. 2025 - Global BESS deployments soared 53% in 2024
  • Lithium Junior Miners News For The Month Of January 2025

Fastmarkets spodumene price forecast 2024-2034 - Prices forecast to improve from 2026 onwards (source)

Conclusion

The lithium junior miners (ie: those not yet in production) have had a torrid past 2.5 years as lithium prices collapsed and sentiment deteriorated towards the sector. This has resulted in some extremely low valuations for most lithium juniors.

Looking ahead to the next few years there is a strong possibility that new lithium projects or expansions get delayed. Assuming very strong lithium demand continues then we may get a more balanced lithium market from 2026 to 2028 and potentially large deficits from 2029 onwards. Any demand surge from the new ~US$28,00 Tesla (TSLA) Model Q or other cheaper EVs in the West may bring forward these deficits to occur sooner rather than later.

This sets up very nicely for patient investors willing to buy and hold the quality lithium juniors for the next 5-7 years plus. Yes some will fail, but those that can succeed have potential for very large returns.

Please read the risks section.

As usual all comments are welcome.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MAXI: Long Bitcoin With A 32% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

In my quest to build the perfect portfolio, I have found a desire to include Bitcoin (BTC-USD) exposure, at least in a very small amount. I most recently discussed Bitcoin's place in a portfolio in an article published in December. That article covers my multi-asset portfolio that uses leverage to enhance returns. It was a fun write, and I recommend taking a look if you're curious about how I justify Bitcoin's inclusion.

In the pursuit of the "perfect" allocation to Bitcoin, I've stumbled upon several funds that utilize interesting strategies, leveraging the availability of Bitcoin derivatives to create income-producing funds that also offer long exposure to Bitcoin itself.

The one that captivated me the most, and that I employ in that portfolio, is the Simplify Bitcoin Strategy PLUS Income ETF (NASDAQ:MAXI).

Over its lifetime, it has performed well, producing significant yield. Its price return since its launch in Q4 2022 is nearly 115%, but its total return (including dividends paid, but not reinvested) is over 310%.

While this doesn't compare to Bitcoin itself, necessarily, the income paid to investors during that time could be worthwhile to many investors.

Consider that its trailing yield is currently almost 32%, and it's fairly impressive to see that it's kept up with long-only exposure.

Strategy

The fund works off of several moving parts, which may seem complex at first, but are fairly straightforward as individual components.

Here is how Simplify explains it:

The Simplify Bitcoin Strategy PLUS Income ETF (NASDAQ:MAXI) seeks capital gains through an actively managed Bitcoin strategy. A risk-managed options overlay is added to provide income.The Bitcoin exposure will vary between 50% to 200% of the fund’s net assets, based on a proprietary technical model. Exposure may be gained through various instruments, including Bitcoin ETFs, futures, options, or swaps.The options income strategy will focus on selling put spreads on a variety of instruments, including equity indices as well as bond and commodity ETFs.**The fund does not invest in bitcoin directly.

Piece One: Treasuries As Collateral

The fund first buys treasuries, with effectively 100% of its original NAV. These treasuries are used as collateral to buy Bitcoin exposure through futures, as well as to sell options spreads.

This makes the base of the fund look like this:

Currently, about 90% of assets are tied up in T-Bills.

Options and futures are capital efficient, however, meaning that they can be held using existing assets as collateral. This means that the fund maintains its 90-100% allocation to T-Bills no matter what else is does in the fund, since the rest of its operations are all derivative-based.

Having the allocation to MAXI in my portfolio contribute to my allocations to Bitcoin, T-Bills, and an options-income strategy was very enticing, and it allows me to do more with less; instead of spreading my allotted allocation across three ETFs doing these.

Piece Two: Options Spreads

Simplify has several partnerships with hedge funds and other investments advisors, and it's unclear if their options-selling algorithm is entirely in-house or not, but they employ a machine to trade options spreads. They explain it this way:

The risk-managed option overlay sells put spreads on a variety of instruments to generate income. This predominantly includes spreads on equity indices as well as on fixed income, commodity, volatility, and currency ETFs and exchange traded products (ETPs), and on Bitcoin futures.

This means that their short exposure will be varied, and not tied directly to Bitcoin. That is a positive thing for MAXI investors as it means that the income is not directly tied to the capping of upside on Bitcoin itself, but rather volatility across other instruments that may not correlate with Bitcoin's returns. This is unlike other Bitcoin income funds that cap the upside of Bitcoin in order to generate its income. MAXI, on the other hand, currently has no options spreads on Bitcoin itself, although they do have positions open against Microstrategy (MSTR), which trades in great sympathy with Bitcoin.

Their options strategy is to open put spreads on various instruments, to take advantage of mispricing opportunities that arise in these assets. It's a black box model, so we can't predict or forecast how the fund will perform on an income front, but here are its total holdings if it helps anybody make sense of their strategy.

Note that the image above also includes a 7.31% allocation to IBIT calls, which are technically options, but are not part of the options-selling program and are instead part of the next component, the long Bitcoin exposure.

Piece Three: Bitcoin Exposure

Keeping in mind that IBIT call allocation enhances the exposure to Bitcoin, the fund also keeps a more "traditional" Bitcoin exposure tool, CME futures. Commodities futures are typically what comes to mind when funds use the words "Bitcoin strategy," as it is not technically investing in "physical" Bitcoin. Instead, it's invested in synthetic Bitcoin, which are backed by cash transactions and not the delivery of actual Bitcoins.

This is not a problem for me, but may be for some folks who want "pure" exposure to Bitcoin without the worry of having to roll futures contracts and deal with the issues that come along with that, namely "carry," or the cost paid for the leverage obtained.

That carry is expensive, and costs MAXI over 500bp in fees annually.

Dynamic Exposure to Bitcoin

The keen-eyed among you may have noticed that the total exposure to Bitcoin was about 57% of NAV currently. This is intentionally less than 100%. Let's call back to the original explainer of this strategy for a reminder:

The Bitcoin exposure will vary between 50% to 200% of the fund’s net assets, based on a proprietary technical model.

Back on December 31st, 2024, Simplify reported MAXI's holdings at over 100% Bitcoin exposure. They have since scaled back, based on their technical readings of the market.

This model is designed to reduce the volatility from holding Bitcoin. Does it work? In the chart below, we can see that MAXI tends to be more volatile than a "pure" Bitcoin fund like IBIT. This is likely due to the volatility from the options, as well as over-exposure to Bitcoin during drawdowns. The volatility diverged dramatically in December last year, but MAXI's is quickly falling back in line.

Because of the other moving parts of the fund, it's unclear if MAXI has the ability to be less volatile than just owning Bitcoin, since some of these fluctuations could be from the put spreads. This is the most major red flag I see with this fund, is that it is not actually less volatile than owning Bitcoin outright.

This strategy has promise, however, as its performance has kept up with Bitcoin over time despite its crazy high yield, over 30%.

Suitability

MAXI is an interesting fund, and one that I believe has great potential as a high-yield way to own Bitcoin, with performance similar to the asset, while still producing 30%+ in income.

There are some red flags, however, such as the high ER from the use of leverage, and the leverage itself. Some investors may be more comfortable owning funds with less "black box" components, or less active management. Volatility is also very high, even higher than Bitcoin outright at times due to the income mechanism.

To this end, I am recommending that investors very carefully consider MAXI when including it in a portfolio. Aggressive investors could consider up to a 3% allocation in a diversified portfolio, and moderate investors could consider up to a 1% allocation. Conservative investors are cautioned to stay away from cryptocurrencies entirely, let alone Bitcoin funds that sell options like MAXI.

In my own portfolio, it holds a 2.5% allocation. You can check that out here.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the Simplify Bitcoin Strategy PLUS Income ETF (MAXI) has impressed me. While it has black box components that make it difficult to predict and forecast, these also contribute to its potential for outperformance. The fund itself has kept up with Bitcoin's performance while distributing a trailing return of 30%+ in income.

This fund may be very appealing to income investors, especially those interested in having Bitcoin exposure. The strategy barbells Bitcoin with T-Bills and adds an aggressive options overlay that does not materially decrease Bitcoin exposure. This means that compared to other Bitcoin income funds, MAXI should receive more of Bitcoin's upside, as it does not cap it for its income returns. In exchange, MAXI can be volatile in ways that Bitcoin isn't, as it sells options on commodities and equities.

Investors should heed caution when considering MAXI, and allocate capital appropriately for their risk tolerance, but it earns a buy rating from me as someone who was previously fairly coin-shy.

Thanks for reading.
